action", "max": null, "options": [], "section_id": "one-page-business-underwriting-conclusion-f0cc0d0a"}], "schema_version": 1, "section_count": 34, "sections": [{"body_markdown": "Purpose: decide whether the business itself is strong enough, understandable enough, and durable enough to justify continuation to **Management Underwriting**, **Financial Underwriting**, and **Valuation and Position Size**.\n\nThis is the second stage of the funnel:\n\n1. Screening\n2. Business Underwriting\n3. Management Underwriting\n4. Financial Underwriting\n5. Valuation and Position Size\n6. Execution Rules\n\nThis document is only for **Business Underwriting**. It is not a management memo, not a balance-sheet memo, not a valuation memo, and not a buy decision. Its job is to prove or disprove the business itself: market boundary, customer value proposition, value chain, profit-pool position, source of competitive advantage, operating statistics, unit economics, capital intensity, reinvestment logic, capital-cycle risk, and competitive destruction risk.\n\nThis document is designed to be used **after Screening**, not instead of Screening. It should deepen the passed business claims, not repeat the stage-1 screen.", "fields": [], "id": "stock-candidate-business-underwriting-questionnaire-v2-162b399b", "level": 1, "title": "Stock Candidate Business Underwriting Questionnaire v2"}, {"body_markdown": "- **Pass Business Underwriting** means \"continue deeper underwriting,\" not \"buy.\"\n- **Watchlist** means \"the business may be interesting, but one or more business claims remain unproven.\"\n- **Archive** means \"stop unless the business, evidence, or price changes materially.\"\n- Start from the **screening handoff**, not from a blank sheet.\n- Use the same evidence grades and confidence tags as the Screening Questionnaire.\n- Do not re-answer stage-1 questions unless Business Underwriting changes the answer materially.\n- Business Underwriting answers business questions only: why customers buy, why they stay, where the profits sit, why competitors fail, what capital the business really needs, and how the economics break.\n- Management claims are not business proof. Use competitor, customer, supplier, channel, trade, and operating evidence whenever possible.\n- Define the market boundary narrowly enough to make competition real.\n- A moat is not a label. Tie every claimed advantage to a mechanism: **supply advantage**, **demand advantage / customer captivity**, **scale advantage**, **regulatory protection**, **network standard**, or **local dominance**.\n- Distinguish a true **franchise** from a good period, a shortage, a favorable cycle, or a well-marketed commodity.\n- Distinguish **economic goodwill** from accounting goodwill and from vague brand affection.\n- Growth is not automatically good. Obvious market growth does not guarantee attractive profits.\n- High returns attract capital. Ask what happens if capacity expands, channels shift, or rivals copy.\n- If the business cannot be understood after proper segmentation, competitor work, and field evidence, classify it **Too Hard**.\n- Preserve downstream questions for later stages; do not let later stages solve a business problem that should be solved here.\n- Do not use a score. Scores create false comparability across unlike businesses.\n- The final output must state the next action in the funnel.\n\nTarget depth:\n\n- **Archive**: often 45-90 minutes is enough once the core business flaw is found.\n- **Watchlist**: usually 2-5 hours across filings, competitors, channels, and external evidence.\n- **Pass Business Underwriting**: enough work to explain the source of advantage, profit pool, operating proof, capital needs, reinvestment logic, and failure modes. Still short of full management, financial, and valuation underwriting.\n\n---", "fields": [{"help": "", "id": "core-rules-4b9f2661-business-underwriting-answers-business-questions-only-1-a22d5149", "kind": "text", "label": "Business Underwriting answers business questions only", "max": null, "options": [], "section_id": "core-rules-4b9f2661"}, {"help": "", "id": "core-rules-4b9f2661-a-moat-is-not-a-label-tie-every-claimed-advantage-to-a-mechanism-1-fed3d925", "kind": "text", "label": "A moat is not a label. Source Of Competitive Advantage"}, {"body_markdown": "Questions:\n\n- Does the company produce or deliver at lower cost than relevant rivals?\n- If yes, why: location, procurement, know-how, process, utilization, asset base, route density, or experience curve?\n- Is the advantage durable, or can competitors match it with time and capital?\n- Is the cost advantage local, segment-specific, or enterprise-wide?\n- What operating evidence supports the claim?\n\nRule:\n\n- **Pass** if a real and defensible cost or supply advantage is evidenced.\n- **Watchlist** if the advantage may exist but the mechanism is still unproven.\n- **Archive** if the supposed cost advantage is just temporary underutilization elsewhere, favorable cycle, or unsupported management language.\n\n**Result**: Pass / the mechanism is still vague.\n- **Archive** if \"scale\" is being used as a slogan rather than a demonstrated economic advantage.\n\n**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive / Not Scale Advantage"}, {"body_markdown": "Questions:\n\n- Does the company enjoy local dominance in a geography, product niche, route system, or installed base?\n- Does it have a partial monopoly in service, spare parts, upgrades, consumables, data, certification, or maintenance?\n- Are the economics monopoly-like only in some countries, channels, or product lines?\n- Does regulation protect the economics, cap the economics, or both?\n- Is market share analysis more informative locally than globally?\n\nRule:\n\n- **Pass** if local dominance, partial monopoly, or regulation creates durable templates.description: Editable starter template for management underwriting.
templates.markdown:
# Management Underwriting Template

Purpose: judge whether management behaves like rational long-term owners.

## Basic Inputs

- Company:
- Ticker:
- Date:
- Analyst:
- Annual letters reviewed:
- Proxy statements reviewed:
- Earnings calls reviewed:

---

## Capital Allocation

- How has management allocated capital across reinvestment, acquisitions, buybacks, dividends, and debt reduction?

**Answer**:

- Have acquisitions created per-share value?

**Answer**:

- Were buybacks done at sensible prices?

**Answer**:

**Score**:

---

## Candor and Accountability

- Does management discuss mistakes and bad news plainly?

**Answer**:

- Are segment economics, obligations, and risks clear enough to judge?

**Answer**:

- Does communication avoid promotional language?

**Answer**:

**Result**: Pass / Borderline / Fail

---

## Incentives and Governance

- Is compensation tied to per-share value creation and long-term returns?

**Answer**:

- Does management own meaningful stock purchased with its own money?

**Answer**:

- Are there related-party, governance, or dilution concerns?

**Answer**:

**Result**: Pass / Borderline / Fail

---

## Management Underwriting Decision

- Primary management strength:
- Primary management concern:
- Red flags:
- Watchlist conditions:
- Recommended reassessment date:

**Summary**:

**Final Decision**: Proceed to Next Step / Watchlist / Archive

templates.description: Imported from the approved management underwriting questionnaire.
templates.markdown:
# Stock Candidate Management Underwriting Questionnaire v1

Purpose: decide whether management deserves entry into **Financial Underwriting**, belongs on a watchlist with a defined trigger, or should be archived.

This is the third stage of the funnel:

1. Screening
2. Business Underwriting
3. Management Underwriting
4. Financial Underwriting
5. Valuation and Position Size
6. Execution Rules

This document is only for **Management Underwriting**. It is not a business memo, not a full financial memo, not a valuation memo, and not a buy decision. Its job is to judge whether the people allocating capital and representing owners are rational, candid, disciplined, and trustworthy enough to deserve the next funnel stage.

This document is designed to be used **after Business Underwriting**, not instead of Business Underwriting. It should deepen the human, incentive, governance, and capital-allocation questions left open by earlier stages. It should not repeat market boundary, moat mechanism, unit economics, or full financial normalization unless management behavior materially changes the answer.

## Core Rules

- **Pass Management Underwriting** means "open Financial Underwriting," not "buy."
- **Watchlist** means "potentially investable, but a specific management fact, action, or change is missing."
- **Archive** means "stop unless management behavior, incentives, governance, or stewardship materially changes."
- Start from the **Business Underwriting handoff**, not from a blank sheet.
- Do not re-answer stage-1 or stage-2 business questions unless management behavior invalidates the earlier answer.
- Judge management on decisions made under discretion: capital allocation, disclosure, compensation, governance, hiring, firing, decentralization, and crisis response.
- Character outranks charm, narrative skill, and short-term EPS.
- Start with behavior, not biography. Favor what management has done over what management says.
- Judge management by per-share value creation, not company size, reported EPS optics, or deal volume.
- Buybacks are good only when price and value make them good.
- Stock-based compensation is a real cost, not a harmless add-back.
- Candor is tested in bad periods, not good periods.
- A strong business can still be a bad investment if managers misuse capital.
- Boards are judged by behavior, not by the word "independent" in a proxy.
- If you would not trust this team with your whole net worth in a private company, do not trust them with a public one.
- Do not use a single score. Some failures are disqualifying regardless of strengths elsewhere.
- Preserve downstream questions for later stages; do not let Financial Underwriting or Valuation solve a management problem that belongs here.
- The final output must state the next action in the funnel.

Target depth:

- **Archive**: often 45-90 minutes is enough if the problem is obvious.
- **Watchlist**: usually 2-4 hours with proxy, letters, calls, capital-allocation casework, and external management evidence.
- **Pass Management Underwriting**: enough work to justify Financial Underwriting, not enough to size a position.

---

# Evidence, Sources, And Confidence

Use the same evidence grades and confidence tags as the Screening and Business Underwriting questionnaires.

Use evidence grades beside important claims.

- **F** = filed, reported, or directly disclosed fact
- **O** = observable behavior, operating action, or externally checkable evidence
- **M** = management claim
- **I** = analyst inference
- **V** = item to verify later

Use confidence tags beside important judgments.

- **High** = supported by several reliable facts or a long relevant record
- **Medium** = plausible, but one important item needs more evidence
- **Low** = early read, thin evidence, or material uncertainty

Rule: if a management decision depends mainly on **M**, **I**, or **V**, the stock normally belongs on the Watchlist, not Pass Management Underwriting.

Minimum source expectations for **Pass Management Underwriting**:

- Business Underwriting document and handoff reviewed
- Latest proxy or remuneration filing
- Latest annual report or 10-K
- At least three years of shareholder letters or equivalent management communications
- At least four earnings calls, including at least one difficult period if available
- Major buyback, issuance, dividend, debt, acquisition, and divestiture actions reviewed against contemporaneous management language
- Insider ownership and insider transaction review
- Board, related-party, control, and governance review
- Some external management evidence beyond company-prepared language when the management claim matters

If these are not reviewed, Pass Management Underwriting requires an explicit reason.

---

# Inherited From Business Underwriting

Use the Business Underwriting Questionnaire as an input, not as duplicated work.

- Company:
- Ticker:
- Date:
- Analyst:
- Business Underwriting document reviewed:
- Business Underwriting decision:
- Business Underwriting date:
- Business type from Business Underwriting:
- Main business claim proved:
- Source of competitive advantage already underwritten:
- Unit-economics read inherited from Business Underwriting:
- Capital-intensity read inherited from Business Underwriting:
- Main business fragility already identified:
- Main question handed to Management Underwriting:
- Main downstream issues already parked for later stages:

Management Underwriting-specific inputs:

- Key executives underwritten:
- Proxy or remuneration years reviewed:
- Letters and call years reviewed:
- Difficult period reviewed:
- Management meetings completed:
- External management sources reviewed:
- Capital-allocation actions case-reviewed:
- Related-party history reviewed:
- Minority-holder or control structure reviewed:
- Other sources reviewed:

---

# Part I. Business Handoff And Delta Thesis

The purpose of this section is to make stage 3 additive. If this section is weak, stop and fix the handoff before doing more work.

- Which exact management claim from Business Underwriting is being tested now?

**Answer**:

- What business facts are already considered proved and should not be re-underwritten here?

**Answer**:

- What would disprove continuation from Business Underwriting?

**Answer**:

- Which questions are explicitly out of scope until Financial Underwriting, Valuation and Position Size, or Execution Rules?

**Answer**:

- What changed, if anything, between Business Underwriting and the start of this stage?

**Answer**:

**Result**: Clear / Needs Clarification / Stop Here

---

# Part II. Fast Kill Screen

Use this first. If a hard Archive answer appears, stop unless there is a specific reason to keep the company on a watchlist.

| Question | Answer | Default Action |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Can I identify who really controls capital allocation and culture? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Archive |
| Is there clear evidence of honesty, candor, and owner orientation? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Archive |
| Would I be comfortable being a silent partner with this team for five to ten years? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Archive |
| Has management avoided clear per-share value destruction in buybacks, issuance, acquisitions, or leverage? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Archive |
| Are incentives and compensation aligned enough with long-term owner outcomes? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Archive |
| Is there no material governance, related-party, dilution, or control abuse? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Archive |
| Is management depth adequate enough that key-person risk is not dominant? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist; No with severe dependence = Archive |
| Do the earlier-stage business claims still survive after the management read? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Archive or return to Business Underwriting |
| Does this stage clearly point to Financial Underwriting next, not to a management rescue story? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Archive |

**Fast Kill Result**: Continue / Watchlist / Archive

**If Archive, primary reason**:

---

# Part III. External Management Evidence And Interaction Log

A Pass result normally needs management evidence beyond polished company language.

## 1. Evidence Coverage Check

- Proxy or remuneration filing fully reviewed? Yes / No
- At least one bad-period call or letter reviewed? Yes / No
- External management evidence from former employees, competitors, customers, suppliers, investors, or industry observers obtained? Yes / No
- Direct management interaction completed? Yes / No
- Major capital-allocation actions compared with contemporaneous words? Yes / No
- Any important management claim still depends mainly on management language? Yes / No

## 2. Evidence Log

| Source type | Person / source | Management question tested | What it said or did | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | Evidence Grade | Confidence |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Filing / proxy / letter |  |  |  | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | F/O/M/I/V | High / Medium / Low |
| Filing / proxy / letter |  |  |  | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | F/O/M/I/V | High / Medium / Low |
| Direct management interaction |  |  |  | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | F/O/M/I/V | High / Medium / Low |
| Former employee / executive |  |  |  | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | F/O/M/I/V | High / Medium / Low |
| Competitor / supplier / customer |  |  |  | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | F/O/M/I/V | High / Medium / Low |
| Board / investor / industry observer |  |  |  | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | F/O/M/I/V | High / Medium / Low |
| Capital-allocation action |  |  |  | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | F/O/M/I/V | High / Medium / Low |
| Other |  |  |  | Supports / Weakens / Mixed | F/O/M/I/V | High / Medium / Low |

## 3. Interaction Read

- Which answer or action most increased trust?

**Answer**:

- Which answer or action most reduced trust?

**Answer**:

- What did management avoid answering directly?

**Answer**:

- Which important management claim still lacks external proof?

**Answer**:

**Evidence Result**: Adequate / Thin / One-Sided / Promotional

---

# Part IV. Hard Management Gates

Each hard gate should result in **Pass**, **Watchlist**, **Archive**. A Watchlist result is acceptable only if the uncertainty is specific and verifiable.

## 1. Integrity, Candor, And Accessibility

Questions:

- Do letters and calls discuss mistakes and bad news plainly?
- Does management talk freely when things are going badly, or does it clam up?
- Are metrics consistent, or does management shift goalposts?
- Is there promotional language, blame shifting, or guidance theater?
- Are adjusted metrics, KPIs, or footnotes hiding economic reality?
- When asked a hard question, does management answer it, dodge it, or delegate it away?

Rule:

- **Pass** if management reports as if owners are partners and no material credibility issue remains.
- **Watchlist** if candor is mixed but the problem is narrow, nonfatal, and verifiable.
- **Archive** if management is materially promotional, evasive, inaccessible, or misleading.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 2. Rational Capital Allocation And Countercyclicality

Questions:

- Does management explain reinvestment, dividends, buybacks, debt, acquisitions, and cash balances as opportunity-cost decisions?
- Has management likely created at least one dollar of per-share value per dollar retained over time?
- When internal reinvestment returns are low, does capital get redirected rationally?
- Is management willing to do nothing when no high-return use exists?
- Has management acted countercyclically when others were procyclical?
- Does management sacrifice short-term EPS optics when long-term owner value requires it?

Rule:

- **Pass** if capital allocation appears rational, owner-oriented, and grounded in per-share value.
- **Watchlist** if the record is mixed but the uncertainty is specific and testable.
- **Archive** if retained capital is habitually misused, procyclically squandered, or defended with weak logic.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 3. Buybacks, Issuance, And Dilution Discipline

Questions:

- Have buybacks been concentrated when shares were likely below value?
- Have repurchases merely offset stock-based compensation or acquisition dilution?
- Has stock been issued only when the company received at least as much business value as it gave?
- Do management comments focus on per-share intrinsic value rather than EPS optics?
- Are options, convertibles, and share issuance treated like selling part of the business?

Rule:

- **Pass** if buybacks and issuance likely increased per-share value or at least did not materially destroy it.
- **Watchlist** if the record is uncertain and needs price/value or dilution work.
- **Archive** if management routinely repurchases overpriced stock, issues undervalued stock, or hides dilution behind adjusted metrics.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 4. Incentives, Compensation, And Agency

Questions:

- Are bonus and equity metrics tied to per-share value, return on capital, owner earnings, or other useful long-term measures?
- Are metrics easy to game through leverage, acquisitions, buybacks, or accounting?
- Are incentives measured over a long enough period to discourage short-termism?
- Is management ownership meaningful and preferably built with purchased shares rather than only granted stock?
- Is stock-based compensation a tool, or a permanent transfer from owners to employees?
- Are downside, clawback, or deferral features real enough to matter?

Rule:

- **Pass** if incentives point broadly toward long-term owner value and management has meaningful owner exposure.
- **Watchlist** if compensation is imperfect but the problem is narrow and fixable.
- **Archive** if incentives materially reward size, optics, leverage, or dilution at owner expense.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 5. Governance, Control, And Minority-Holder Treatment

Questions:

- Are directors meaningfully independent in behavior, not merely on paper?
- If the company is family-controlled, founder-controlled, state-influenced, or dual-class, have minority owners historically been treated fairly?
- Are related-party transactions, unequal treatment, takeover defenses, or control features owner-friendly or owner-hostile?
- Does the board challenge management on pay, deals, leverage, and succession?
- Are owners treated as partners or as counterparties to be managed?

Rule:

- **Pass** if governance appears owner-protective and no material abuse is visible.
- **Watchlist** if governance is imperfect but the risk is bounded and specific.
- **Archive** if owners are materially disadvantaged by governance structure, controller behavior, or board passivity.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 6. Management Depth, Delegation, And Culture

Questions:

- Is there credible CEO and CFO succession?
- Is authority delegated intelligently, or hoarded at the top?
- Are promising younger executives being developed in real depth?
- Does management welcome contrary suggestions and bad news from below?
- Would culture, capital allocation, and reporting standards likely survive key-person loss?
- Are strong operators empowered, or does everything depend on one hero?

Rule:

- **Pass** if key-person risk is acceptable, authority is delegated sensibly, and the management system looks durable.
- **Watchlist** if depth is unclear but the issue is temporary or learnable.
- **Archive** if the investment case depends on a single irreplaceable person, weak delegation, or a culture too fragile to survive strain.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 7. Resistance To The Institutional Imperative And Empire Building

Questions:

- Does management copy peers on leverage, acquisitions, guidance, or compensation, or act independently?
- Has the company ever shrunk, paused growth, exited a bad business, or returned cash when the crowd was still expanding?
- Are banker, consultant, or board incentives obviously steering decisions?
- Does management prefer activity over rational inactivity?
- Is management trying to build value, or trying to build a larger empire?

Rule:

- **Pass** if management shows independent judgment and can resist fashionable but value-destructive behavior.
- **Watchlist** if the team may be rational but needs one or two behavior checks.
- **Archive** if lemming-like behavior or size worship clearly dominates decision making.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

---

# Part V. Management Quality Snapshot

Do not score. Rate each item: **Strong / Adequate / Weak / Unknown**.

| Item | Rating | Evidence Grade | Source | Confidence | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Owner orientation |  | F/O/M/I/V |  | High / Medium / Low |  |
| Candor in bad periods |  | F/O/M/I/V |  | High / Medium / Low |  |

# Part VI. Capital Allocation Case File

## 1. Capital Allocation Authority And Constraints

- Who decides on reinvestment, acquisitions, buybacks, dividends, leverage, and issuance?

**Answer**:

- What formal hurdle rates, valuation rules, or guardrails does management claim to use?

**Answer**:

- Which decisions require board approval?

**Answer**:

- Does the company describe capital allocation in owner terms or accounting terms?

**Answer**:

**Result**: Clear / Mixed / Promotional / Unknown

## 2. Capital Allocation Timeline

## 3. Retained-Capital Test

- Approximate cumulative capital retained over last 5 years:
- Approximate per-share value, owner earnings, or share-count benefit created:
- Major dilution from issuance, options, or convertibles:
- Rough value created per dollar retained:

Questions:

- Has management likely created at least one dollar of value per dollar retained over time?
- If not, should capital have been paid out, used for buybacks, or not raised in the first place?
- Is the test clearly passed, borderline, failed, or still unknowable?

**Result**: Strong / Adequate / Weak / Unknown

## 4. Buyback And Issuance Review

- When did the company repurchase most aggressively?

**Answer**:

- What was the valuation context during major repurchase periods?

**Answer**:

- Were repurchases opportunistic, formulaic, or anti-dilutive only?

**Answer**:

- Major issuance, option, or convertible events:
- Did management explain issuance as if selling part of the business?

Questions:

- Did buybacks increase per-share value?
- Did management avoid issuing undervalued stock?
- Are EPS optics hiding poor per-share economics?

**Result**: Strong / Adequate / Weak / Unknown

## 5. Acquisition, Divestiture, And Shutdown Record

- Is there evidence of "do something" bias or size worship?
- Were acquisitions small and digestible, or large and faith-based?

**Result**: Strong / Adequate / Weak / Unknown

## 6. Risk Appetite And Balance Sheet Behavior

- Major leverage-increasing decisions:
- Major deleveraging decisions:

- Evidence management chased return with leverage:

- Did management act prudently in bad periods?

# Part VIII. What Must Be True / What To Verify

This replaces vague "management quality" claims. Do not keep researching just because management seems impressive. List the few things that must be true for management to deserve Financial Underwriting.

Good examples:

- Reported buyback discipline is real and price-sensitive.
- Stock-based compensation is not a permanent hidden transfer from owners.
- Recent acquisition returns are not overstated.
- The board will restrain value-destructive empire building.
- CEO candor in recent calls reflects culture, not temporary pressure.
- Succession is credible enough that key-person risk does not dominate the case.

Use no more than seven items. If the thesis needs more than seven major management uncertainties to go right, it is probably too fragile for this stage.

| What must be true? | Current evidence | Source | Confidence | Funnel stage to verify | How to verify |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | F/O/M/I/V |  | High / Medium / Low | Management / Financial / Valuation / Execution |  |
|  | F/O/M/I/V |  | High / Medium / Low | Management / Financial / Valuation / Execution |  |

|  | F/O/M/I/V |  | High / Medium / Low | Management / Financial / Valuation / Execution |  |

**Fragility Read**: Robust / Some Fragility / Highly Fragile / Too Many Unknowns

# Part IX. Munger Error-Prevention Checks

## 1. Inversion

- How could this management destroy a good business?

**Answer**:

- What is the strongest reason management is overrated?

**Answer**:

- What behavior would cause permanent capital loss even if the business is real?

**Answer**:

- What would make this an immediate Archive?

**Answer**:

- What am I most likely rationalizing because the business itself is attractive?

**Answer**:

## 2. Psychology And Bias Audit

Check any that apply.

- I am giving management credit because the business is high quality.
- I am overweighting charisma, founder myth, or reputation.
- I am confusing high historical returns with current capital allocation skill.
- I am excusing poor buybacks because the stock later went up.
- I am ignoring dilution because it is called non-cash.
- I am accepting peer pay practices as normal just because peers do it.
- I am treating detailed slides as evidence of candor.
- I am assuming a famous board is a strong board.
- I am giving a pass because the CEO owns stock that was mostly granted, not bought.
- I am letting recent price weakness or strength color the management view.
- I am assuming the next stage will solve a real trust problem.
- I am ignoring that incentives point the wrong way.

Most important bias risk:

**Answer**:

## 3. Incentive-Caused Bias

- What incentive is most likely to impair management's judgment?

**Answer**:

- What incentive is most likely to impair the board's judgment?

**Answer**:

- Where might my own incentive as an analyst be contaminating the read?

**Answer**:

## 4. Base Rates And Outside View

- What is the base rate for serial acquirers in this industry creating per-share value?

**Answer**:

- What usually happens after equity-heavy compensation plans expand?

**Answer**:

- What usually happens when management buys back heavily at high multiples?

**Answer**:

- What usually happens when founder-led or controller-led companies lose the key person?

**Answer**:

- What is the base rate for boards meaningfully restraining empire building?

**Answer**:

- If this team is claimed to be exceptional, what facts actually justify the exception?

**Answer**:

**Munger Check Result**: Clear / Needs Verification / Archive

---

# Part X. Management Category And Next Underwriting Standard

Pick the category based on behavior, not presentation skill. Do not use a criteria count mechanically.

## Category Options

### Exceptional Owner-Operators

Traits:

- High integrity
- Strong owner orientation
- Excellent capital allocation record
- Sensible buybacks and issuance
- Candid communication
- Real independence of thought
- Thoughtful succession and low bureaucracy

Management standard:

- Can pass with smaller imperfections only if the record is long and governance is clean.

Financial Underwriting must prove next:

- Reported returns are real.
- Owner earnings are clean.
- No hidden liabilities or accounting offsets the management quality.

### Rational Stewards

Traits:

- Capable operators
- Sensible if not exceptional capital allocation
- Adequate candor
- Aligned enough incentives
- Acceptable governance
- Some owner orientation

Management standard:

- Can pass if no fatal misalignment remains and the per-share record is acceptable.

Financial Underwriting must prove next:

- Retained capital economics are real.
- Cash conversion supports reported quality.
- Leverage and stock-based compensation do not erode owner returns.

### Capable But Agency-Risky

Traits:

- Decent operators
- Mixed capital allocation
- Material compensation, buyback, issuance, or acquisition concerns
- Governance may be serviceable but not protective

Management standard:

- Usually Watchlist unless the misalignment is narrow, verifiable, and fixable.

Financial Underwriting must prove next:

- Whether the economics are strong enough to offset the management discount.
- Whether dilution, leverage, or acquisition accounting hides weak decisions.

### Promotional / Empire-Building

Traits:

- Narrative-heavy communication
- Deal activity favored over value
- Peer imitation
- EPS optics over per-share value
- Large pay with weak owner outcomes

Management standard:

- Archive unless there is clear and already visible change.

Next step:

- Do not proceed in the normal funnel without concrete evidence of a new capital allocation regime.

### Control / Governance Risk

Traits:

- Related-party issues
- Unequal treatment
- Weak board challenge
- Entrenchment
- Opaque decision making

Management standard:

- Archive unless governance risk is both narrow and remediable.

Next step:

- Use a governance-risk or special-situation process only if the discount is extreme and the risk is clearly bounded.

### Too Hard

Traits:

- Cannot identify the real decision maker
- Incentives are opaque
- Capital allocation record is uninterpretable
- Governance disclosures do not allow an owner-level judgment

Management standard:

- Archive.

**Management Category Result**: Exceptional Owner-Operators / Rational Stewards / Capable But Agency-Risky / Promotional-Empire / Control-Governance Risk / Too Hard

| Management quality | Strong / Adequate / Weak / Unknown | High / Medium / Low |

| Candor and disclosure | Strong / Adequate / Weak / Unknown | High / Medium / Low |
| Governance and incentives | Strong / Adequate / Weak / Unknown | High / Medium / Low |
| Succession and culture | Strong / Adequate / Weak / Unknown | High / Medium / Low |
| Evidence base | Strong / Adequate / Thin / Poor | High / Medium / Low |

## Final Decision

- Decision: Pass Management Underwriting / Watchlist / Archive
- Management category:

- Main risk:

## If It Passes Management Underwriting

Pass Management Underwriting requires:

- Clear Business Underwriting handoff
- No material integrity or governance kill issue
- Rational capital allocation record or a narrow explainable gap
- Candor in bad periods
- Incentives and ownership aligned enough with per-share value
- No clear buyback, issuance, acquisition, or leverage abuse
- Management depth and culture not fatally fragile
- Specific Financial Underwriting tasks

Write the handoff to **Financial Underwriting**:

3.

Preserve, but do not solve yet, the key downstream issues:

- Residual management monitoring issue:
- Financial Underwriting issue:
- Valuation and Position Size issue:
- Execution Rules issue:

## If It Goes To Watchlist

Select one or more labels and define the trigger.

- **Need More External Management Evidence**: scuttlebutt, reference checks, or direct management interaction still too thin:
- **Capital Allocation Proof**: retained-capital test, post-acquisition evidence, or use-of-cash decision:
- **Buyback / Issuance Discipline**: repurchase behavior, dilution, or stock-financed deal condition:
- **Compensation / Incentive Review**: metric design, stock-based compensation burden, ownership quality, or pay reset:
- **Candor / Disclosure**: letters, calls, KPI consistency, or bad-news handling:
- **Governance / Control Risk**: board change, related-party cleanup, control simplification, or shareholder-rights event:
- **Succession / Key Person**: bench strength, CEO transition, or operating depth:
- **Post-Management Change**: new CEO, CFO, chair, or capital allocation regime:
- **Too Hard For Now**: knowledge required before revisiting:

## If It Is Archived

- Archive reason:
- What would need to change before revisiting?
- Is the issue character-related, incentive-related, governance-related, capital-allocation related, succession-related, or knowledge-related?

"label": "Next action", "max": null, "options": [], "section_id": "one-page-management-conclusion-f9ecf53b"}], "schema_version": 1, "section_count": 55, "sections": [{"body_markdown": "Purpose: decide whether management deserves entry into **Financial Underwriting**, belongs on a watchlist with a defined trigger, or should be archived.\n\nThis is the third stage of the funnel:\n\n1. Screening\n2. Business Underwriting\n3. Management Underwriting\n4. Financial Underwriting\n5. Valuation and Position Size\n6. Execution Rules\n\nThis document is only for **Management Underwriting**. It is not a business memo, not a full financial memo, not a valuation memo, and not a buy decision. Its job is to judge whether the people allocating capital and representing owners are rational, candid, disciplined, and trustworthy enough to deserve the next funnel stage.\n\nThis document is designed to be used **after Business Underwriting**, not instead of Business Underwriting. It should deepen the human, incentive, governance, and capital-allocation questions left open by earlier stages. It should not repeat market boundary, moat mechanism, unit economics, or full financial normalization unless management behavior materially changes the answer.", "fields": [], "id": "stock-candidate-management-underwriting-questionnaire-v1-d0e0b3ff", "level": 1, "title": "Stock Candidate Management Underwriting Questionnaire v1"}, {"body_markdown": "- **Pass Management Underwriting** means \"open Financial Underwriting,\" not \"buy.\"\n- **Watchlist** means \"potentially investable, but a specific management fact, action, or change is missing.\"\n- **Archive** means \"stop unless management behavior, incentives, governance, or stewardship materially changes.\"\n- Start from the **Business Underwriting handoff**, not from a blank sheet.\n- Do not re-answer stage-1 or stage-2 business questions unless management behavior invalidates the earlier answer.\n- Judge management on decisions made under discretion: capital allocation, disclosure, compensation, governance, hiring, firing, decentralization, and crisis response.\n- Character outranks charm, narrative skill, and short-term EPS.\n- Start with behavior, not biography. Favor what management has done over what management says.\n- Judge management by per-share value creation, not company size, reported EPS optics, or deal volume.\n- Buybacks are good only when price and value make them good.\n- Stock-based compensation is a real cost, not a harmless add-back.\n- Candor is tested in bad periods, not good periods.\n- A strong business can still be a bad investment if managers misuse capital.\n- Boards are judged by behavior, not by the word \"independent\" in a proxy.\n- If you would not trust this team with your whole net worth in a private company, do not trust them with a public one.\n- Do not use a single score. Some failures are disqualifying regardless of strengths elsewhere.\n- Preserve downstream questions for later stages; do not let Financial Underwriting or Valuation solve a management problem that belongs here.\n- The final output must state the next action in the funnel.\n\nTarget depth:\n\n- **Archive**: often 45-90 minutes is enough if the problem is obvious.\n- **Watchlist**: usually 2-4 hours with proxy, letters, calls, capital-allocation casework, and external management evidence.\n- **Pass Management Underwriting**: enough work to justify Financial Underwriting, not enough to size a position.\n\n---", "fields": [{"help": "", "id": "core-rules-4b9f2661-judge-management-on-decisions-made-under-discretion-1-92f4f92c", "kind": "text", "label": "Judge management on decisions made under discretion", "max": null, "options": [], "section_id": "core-rules-4b9f2661"}, {"help": "", "id": "core-rules-4b9f2661-archive-1-cedb00c6", "kind": "text", "label": "Archive", "max": null, "core-rules-4b9f2661"}, {"help": "", "id": "core-rules-4b9f2661-pass-management-underwriting-1-92f1a105", "kind": "text", "label": "Pass Management Underwriting", "max": null, "options": [], "section_id": "core-rules-4b9f2661"}], "id": "core-rules-4b9f2661", "level": 2, "title": "Core Rules"}, {"body_markdown": "Use the same evidence grades and confidence tags as the Screening and Business Underwriting questionnaires.\n\nUse evidence grades beside important claims.\n\n- **F** = filed, reported, or directly disclosed fact\n- **O** = observable behavior, operating action, or externally checkable evidence\n- **M** = management claim\n- **I** = analyst inference\n- **V** = item to verify later\n\nUse confidence tags beside important judgments.\n\n- **High** = supported by several reliable facts or a long relevant record\n- **Medium** = plausible, but one important item needs more evidence\n- **Low** = early read, thin evidence, or material uncertainty\n\nRule: if a management decision depends mainly on **M**, **I**, or **V**, the stock normally belongs on the Watchlist, not Pass Management Underwriting.\n\nMinimum source expectations for **Pass Management Underwriting**:\n\n- Business Underwriting document and handoff reviewed\n- Latest proxy or remuneration filing\n- Latest annual report or 10-K\n- At least three years of shareholder letters or equivalent management communications\n- At least four earnings calls, including at least one difficult period if available\n- Major buyback, issuance, dividend, debt, acquisition, and divestiture actions reviewed against contemporaneous management language\n- Insider ownership and insider transaction review\n- Board, related-party, control, and governance review\n- Some external management evidence beyond company-prepared language when the management claim matters\n\nIf these are not reviewed, Pass Management Underwriting requires an explicit reason.\n\n---", "fields": [], "id": A Watchlist result is acceptable only if the uncertainty is specific and verifiable.", "fields": [], "id": "part-iv-hard-management-gates-35c03704", "level": 1, "title": "Part IV. Hard Management Gates"}, {"body_markdown": "Questions:\n\n- Do letters and calls discuss mistakes and bad news plainly?\n- Does management talk freely when things are going badly, or does it clam up?\n- Are metrics consistent, or does management shift goalposts?\n- Is there promotional language, blame shifting, or guidance theater?\n- Are adjusted metrics, KPIs, or footnotes hiding economic reality?\n- When asked a hard question, does management answer it, dodge it, or delegate it away?\n\nRule:\n\n- **Pass** if management reports as if owners are partners and no material credibility issue remains.\n- **Watchlist** if candor is mixed but the problem is narrow, nonfatal, and verifiable.\n- **Archive** if management is materially promotional, evasive, inaccessible, or misleading.\n\n**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive\n\n**Notes**:", "fields": [{"help": "Questions block", "id": Integrity, Candor, And Accessibility"}, {"body_markdown": "Questions:\n\n- Does management explain reinvestment, dividends, buybacks, debt, acquisitions, and cash balances as opportunity-cost decisions?\n- Has management likely created at least one dollar of per-share value per dollar retained over time?\n- When internal reinvestment returns are low, does capital get redirected rationally?\n- Is management willing to do nothing when no high-return use exists?\n- Has management acted countercyclically when others were procyclical?\n- Does management sacrifice short-term EPS optics when long-term owner value requires it?\n\nRule:\n\n- **Pass** if capital allocation appears rational, owner-oriented, and grounded in per-share value.\n- **Watchlist** if the record is mixed but the uncertainty is specific and testable.\n- **Archive** if retained capital is habitually misused, procyclically squandered, or defended with weak logic.\n\n**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive\n\n**Notes**:", "fields": [{"help": "Questions block", "id": Rational Capital Allocation And Countercyclicality"}, {"body_markdown": "Questions:\n\n- Have buybacks been concentrated when shares were likely below value?\n- Have repurchases merely offset stock-based compensation or acquisition dilution?\n- Has stock been issued only when the company received at least as much business value as it gave?\n- Do management comments focus on per-share intrinsic value rather than EPS optics?\n- Are options, convertibles, and share issuance treated like selling part of the business?\n\nRule:\n\n- **Pass** if buybacks and issuance likely increased per-share value or at least did not materially destroy it.\n- **Watchlist** if the record is uncertain and needs price/value or dilution work.\n- **Archive** if management routinely repurchases measures?\n- Are metrics easy to game through leverage, acquisitions, buybacks, or accounting?\n- Are incentives measured over a long enough period to discourage short-termism?\n- Is management ownership meaningful and preferably built with purchased shares rather than only granted stock?\n- Is stock-based compensation a tool, or a permanent transfer from owners to employees?\n- Are downside, clawback, or deferral features real enough to matter?\n\nRule:\n\n- **Pass** if incentives point broadly toward long-term owner value and management has meaningful owner exposure.\n- **Watchlist** if compensation is imperfect but the problem is narrow and fixable.\n- **Archive** if incentives materially reward size, optics, leverage, or dilution at owner expense.\n\n**Result**: founder-controlled, state-influenced, or dual-class, have minority owners historically been treated fairly?\n- Are related-party transactions, unequal treatment, takeover defenses, or control features owner-friendly or owner-hostile?\n- Does the board challenge management on pay, deals, leverage, and succession?\n- Are owners treated as partners or as counterparties to be managed?\n\nRule:\n\n- **Pass** if governance appears owner-protective and no material abuse is visible.\n- **Watchlist** if governance is imperfect but the risk is bounded and specific.\n- **Archive** if owners are Are promising younger executives being developed in real depth?\n- Does management welcome contrary suggestions and bad news from below?\n- Would culture, capital allocation, and reporting standards likely survive key-person loss?\n- Are strong operators empowered, or does everything depend on one hero?\n\nRule:\n\n- **Pass** if key-person risk is acceptable, authority is delegated sensibly, and the management system looks durable.\n- **Watchlist** if depth is unclear but the issue is temporary or learnable.\n- **Archive** if the investment case depends on a single irreplaceable person, weak resist fashionable but value-destructive behavior.\n- **Watchlist** if the team may be rational but needs one or two behavior checks.\n- **Archive** if lemming-like behavior or size worship clearly Culture And Long-Range Orientation"}, {"body_markdown": "This replaces vague \"management quality\" claims. Do not keep researching just because management seems impressive. List the few things that must be true for management to deserve Financial Underwriting.\n\nGood examples:\n\n- Reported buyback discipline is real and price-sensitive.\n- Stock-based compensation is not a permanent hidden transfer from owners.\n- Recent acquisition returns are not overstated.\n- The board will restrain value-destructive empire building.\n- CEO candor in recent calls reflects culture, not temporary pressure.\n- Succession is credible enough that key-person risk does not dominate the case.\n\nUse no more than seven items. low bureaucracy\n\nManagement standard:\n\n- Can pass with smaller imperfections only if the record is long and governance is clean.\n\nFinancial Underwriting must prove next:\n\n- Reported returns are real.\n- Owner earnings are clean.\n- No hidden liabilities or accounting offsets the management quality.", "fields": [], "id": "exceptional-owner-operators-15b94f0b", "level": 3, "title": "Exceptional Owner-Operators"}, {"body_markdown": "Traits:\n\n- Capable operators\n- Sensible if not exceptional capital allocation\n- Adequate candor\n- Aligned enough incentives\n- Acceptable governance\n- Some owner orientation\n\nManagement standard:\n\n- Can pass if no fatal misalignment remains and the per-share record is acceptable.\n\nFinancial Underwriting must prove next:\n\n- Retained capital economics are real.\n- Cash conversion supports reported quality.\n- Leverage and stock-based compensation do not erode owner returns.", "fields": [], "id": "rational-stewards-cd91a50d", "level": 3, "title": "Rational Stewards"}, {"body_markdown": "Traits:\n\n- Decent operators\n- Mixed capital allocation\n- Material compensation, buyback, issuance, or acquisition concerns\n- Governance may be serviceable but not protective\n\nManagement standard:\n\n- Usually Watchlist unless the misalignment is narrow, verifiable, and fixable.\n\nFinancial Underwriting must prove next:\n\n- Whether the economics are strong enough to offset the management discount.\n- Whether dilution, leverage, or acquisition accounting hides weak decisions.", "fields": [], "id": "capable-but-agency-risky-f44c4b4c", "level": 3, "title": "Capable But Agency-Risky"}, {"body_markdown": "Traits:\n\n- Narrative-heavy communication\n- Deal activity favored over value\n- Peer imitation\n- EPS optics over per-share value\n- Large pay with weak owner outcomes\n\nManagement standard:\n\n- Archive unless there is clear and already visible change.\n\nNext step:\n\n- Do not proceed in the normal funnel without concrete evidence of a new capital allocation regime.", "fields": [], "id": "promotional-empire-building-380850cc", "level": 3, "title": "Promotional / templates.description: Editable starter template for financial underwriting.
templates.markdown:
# Financial Underwriting Template

Purpose: normalize the financials and decide whether the economics are durable and owner-oriented.

## Basic Inputs

- Company:
- Ticker:
- Date:
- Analyst:
- Currency:
- Financial statements reviewed:

---

## Returns on Capital

- 5-year average ROIC:
- 5-year average ROE:
- Return on tangible capital:
- Net debt / EBITDA:

- Are returns high without undue leverage?

**Answer**:

- Are returns stable through cycles?

**Answer**:

**Result**: Pass / Borderline / Fail

---

## Owner Earnings

- Reported earnings:
- Depreciation and amortization:
- Stock-based compensation:
- Maintenance capex estimate:
- Required working-capital investment:
- Normalized owner earnings:
- Owner earnings per share:

- Are owner earnings positive and recurring?

**Answer**:

- Are cash conversion and working-capital swings understandable?

**Answer**:

**Score**:

---

## Balance Sheet and Downturn Resilience

- Interest coverage:
- Debt maturities:
- Fixed-charge coverage:
- Pension, lease, legal, and off-balance-sheet obligations:

- Can the company survive a recession or credit freeze without permanent owner impairment?

**Answer**:

**Result**: Pass / Borderline / Fail

---

## Financial Underwriting Decision

- Main financial strength:
- Main financial weakness:
- Red flags:
- Watchlist conditions:
- Recommended reassessment date:

**Summary**:

**Final Decision**: Proceed to Next Step / Watchlist / Archive

templates.description: Imported from the approved financial underwriting questionnaire.
templates.markdown:
# Stock Candidate Financial Underwriting Questionnaire v1

Purpose: decide whether a stock deserves entry into **Valuation and Position Size**, belongs on a watchlist with a defined financial trigger, should be returned to an earlier underwriting stage, or should be archived.

This is the fourth stage of the funnel:

1. Screening
2. Business Underwriting
3. Management Underwriting
4. Financial Underwriting
5. Valuation and Position Size
6. Execution Rules

This document is only for **Financial Underwriting**. It is not a business memo, not a management memo, not a valuation memo, and not a buy decision. Its job is to turn reported numbers into owner economics, test whether the accounting describes reality, stress the balance sheet, and decide whether the financial record is clean enough to value.

This document is designed to be used **after Management Underwriting**, not instead of Management Underwriting. It should deepen the accounting, cash-flow, capital-structure, and normalization questions left open by earlier stages. It should not repeat market boundary, moat mechanism, culture, or capital-allocation intent unless the numbers materially change those conclusions.

## Core Rules

- **Pass Financial Underwriting** means "open Valuation and Position Size," not "buy."
- **Watchlist** means "the numbers may be good enough later, but one or two specific financial facts or conditions are missing."
- **Archive** means "weak economics, aggressive accounting, or financial fragility create unacceptable permanent-loss risk."
- **Return** means "the apparent financial problem is really a business-quality or management-quality problem and should go back to the correct stage."
- Start from the **Management Underwriting handoff**, not from a blank sheet.
- Do not re-answer stage-1, stage-2, or stage-3 questions unless financial work materially changes the answer.
- Financial Underwriting answers financial questions only: statement reliability, owner earnings, return on capital, working capital, capital structure, dilution math, normalization, and readiness for valuation.
- Use owner earnings, not reported EPS, adjusted EBITDA, or promotional non-GAAP metrics, as the center of the analysis.
- Start with the cash flow statement and balance sheet, then use the income statement as a cross-check.
- Evaluate per-share outcomes, not aggregate growth.
- High returns created by leverage, tax quirks, or accounting cosmetics are not high returns.
- A strong income statement cannot rescue a weak balance sheet.
- A low multiple cannot rescue dishonest accounting, chronic dilution, or value-destructive reinvestment.
- Normalize with evidence. Do not treat temporary working-capital releases, peak margins, reserve releases, or refinancing assumptions as durable economics.
- When a mature business or full cycle history exists, prefer 10-year trend work to 3-5-year snapshots.
- Thresholds are guides, not automatic rules. The burden of proof rises when the business is cyclical, opaque, leveraged, acquisitive, or balance-sheet-sensitive.
- For banks, insurers, and other financials, use the financial/insurer sections and do not force industrial metrics where they do not belong.
- Do not reopen moat, compensation, or acquisition-strategy debates except where those issues show up numerically in returns, dilution, cash generation, or capital structure.
- If the thesis works only on adjusted EBITDA while depreciation, capex, working capital, reserves, or dilution are real, stop.
- Unknowns are not neutral. A major unknown is a reason to Watchlist, Return, or Archive.

Target depth:

- **Archive**: often 45-90 minutes after building a simple financial map.
- **Watchlist**: usually 2-4 hours.
- **Pass Financial Underwriting**: enough work to support valuation, not enough to set a buy price or position size.

---

# Evidence, Sources, And Confidence

Use evidence grades beside important claims.

- **F** = audited, reported, or directly filed fact
- **O** = observable operating, customer, competitive, or industry evidence
- **M** = management claim
- **I** = analyst inference
- **V** = item to verify later

Use confidence tags beside important judgments.

- **High** = supported by several reliable facts or a long relevant record
- **Medium** = plausible, but one important item needs more evidence
- **Low** = early read, thin evidence, or material uncertainty

Rule: if a financial underwriting decision depends mainly on **M**, **I**, or **V**, the stock normally belongs on the Watchlist or should be returned, not Pass Financial Underwriting.

Minimum source expectations for **Pass Financial Underwriting**:

- Latest annual report or 10-K
- Latest quarterly report or 10-Q
- At least 10 years of income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow history when available, and a full-cycle history when the business is cyclical
- Debt maturity, lease, pension, reserve, tax, and stock-compensation notes
- Share-count history and capital-allocation history
- Working-capital detail, including receivables, inventory, payables, contract assets, and deferred revenue where relevant
- Non-GAAP reconciliations, if management uses adjusted metrics
- Capitalization-policy review, including software, R&D, content, implementation, cloud, or customer-acquisition cost treatment where relevant
- Acquisition / intangible / purchase-accounting review if the company is acquisitive
- For financials and insurers: regulatory capital, liquidity, funding, reserve or credit-quality, and asset-liability disclosures

If these are not reviewed, Pass Financial Underwriting requires an explicit reason.

---

# Inherited From Management Underwriting

Use the Management Underwriting Questionnaire as an input, not as duplicated work.

- Company:
- Ticker:
- Date:
- Analyst:
- Management Underwriting document reviewed:
- Management Underwriting decision:
- Management Underwriting date:
- Business type inherited from Business Underwriting:
- Core business model already underwritten:
- Unit-economics and capital-intensity read inherited from Business Underwriting:
- Capital-allocation, dilution, and governance read inherited from Management Underwriting:
- Main financial question handed to this stage:
- Main accounting concern already identified:
- Main balance-sheet risk already identified:
- Main normalization issue already identified:
- Main downstream issues already parked for later stages:

Financial Underwriting-specific setup:

- Historical span underwritten:
- Segment bridge built:
- Diluted share-count bridge built:
- Debt / lease / pension / reserve roll-forwards built:
- Banking / insurer specialist disclosures reviewed, if relevant:
- Other sources reviewed:

---

# Basic Inputs

- Company:
- Ticker:
- Date:
- Analyst:
- Currency:
- Accounting standard:
- Fiscal year-end:
- Current share price:
- Diluted shares:
- Market capitalization:
- Net debt / net cash:
- Enterprise value:
- Latest annual report reviewed:
- Latest quarterly report reviewed:
- Years of history reviewed:
- Debt agreements / maturity schedule reviewed:
- Lease / pension / other long-term obligations reviewed:
- Proxy / compensation filing reviewed for stock compensation:
- Acquisition / goodwill / intangible review completed:
- Segment reporting reviewed:
- Financial model or worksheet used:
- Primary financial sources reviewed:
- Other sources reviewed:

---

# Part I. Management Handoff And Delta Thesis

The purpose of this section is to make stage 4 additive. If this section is weak, stop and fix the handoff before doing more work.

- Which exact financial claim from Screening, Business Underwriting, or Management Underwriting is being tested now?

**Answer**:

- What business and management facts are already considered proved and should not be re-underwritten here?

**Answer**:

- What would disprove continuation from Management Underwriting?

**Answer**:

- Which questions are explicitly out of scope until Valuation and Position Size or Execution Rules?

**Answer**:

- What changed, if anything, between Management Underwriting and the start of this stage?

**Answer**:

- Which issues were deliberately parked for this stage by earlier work?

**Answer**:

**Result**: Clear / Needs Clarification / Stop Here

---

# Part II. Fast Financial Kill Screen

Use this first. If a hard Archive or Return answer appears, stop unless there is a specific reason to keep the company on a watchlist.

| Question | Answer | Default Action |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Can I reconcile the income statement, cash flow statement, and balance sheet well enough to judge the business? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Archive or Return |
| Can owner earnings be approximated without heroic assumptions? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Archive |
| Are returns on capital attractive enough without undue leverage or accounting help? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Archive |
| Does cash conversion broadly support the earnings story over a cycle? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist, Return, or Archive |
| Are non-GAAP adjustments, acquisition add-backs, and "one-time" items limited and understandable? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Archive or Watchlist |
| Are share count, stock-based compensation, and dilution owner-friendly enough? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Return |
| Can the balance sheet survive a bad year without forced dilution or distressed refinancing? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Archive |
| Are off-balance-sheet obligations, pensions, reserves, tax items, or funding risks manageable and understandable? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Archive |
| Has retained or incremental capital likely created value per share? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist or Return |
| Are normalized margins, capex, working capital needs, and owner earnings estimable enough to value later? | Yes / No / Unknown | No = Watchlist |
| For financials and insurers, are capital, funding, reserves or credit, and asset-liability risks understandable? | Yes / No / Unknown / N/A | No = Archive or Watchlist |

**Fast Kill Result**: Continue / Watchlist / Archive / Return

**If Archive or Return, primary reason**:

---

# Part III. Financial Snapshot

## 1. Economics In Plain Numbers

- What reported line or set of lines best tracks the underlying economics of the business?

**Answer**:

- What expenses are unquestionably real even if management adjusts them away?

**Answer**:

- What capital is required to maintain the business at its current competitive position?

**Answer**:

- What capital is required to grow the business?

**Answer**:

- What working-capital pattern matters most to owner earnings?

**Answer**:

- What balance-sheet items could hurt owners despite a healthy-looking income statement?

**Answer**:

- What are the two or three financial variables that matter most to value?

**Answer**:

- What important financial question is still outside my competence?

**Answer**:

**Result**: Clear / Needs Verification / Too Hard

## 2. Why Does This Need Financial Underwriting?

- Why does this company require deeper financial work now?

**Answer**:

- What is the simplest attractive financial read?

**Answer**:

- What is the simplest financial reason to reject it?

**Answer**:

- What would make this a false positive at the financial stage?

**Answer**:

- Is the real issue financial quality, business quality, management behavior, valuation, or some combination?

**Answer**:

---

# Part IV. Hard Financial Gates

Each hard gate should result in **Pass**, **Watchlist**, or **Archive**. A Watchlist result is acceptable only if the uncertainty is specific and verifiable. If the issue clearly belongs to Business or Management Underwriting, note that in the gate and use **Return** in the final decision.

## 1. Accounting Reality And Statement Cleanliness

Questions:

- Do reported results reconcile across the income statement, cash flow statement, and balance sheet?
- Are non-GAAP adjustments narrow, credible, and truly non-recurring?
- Are contract assets, unbilled receivables, deferred revenue, and channel inventory consistent with reported revenue quality?
- Are revenue recognition, capitalization policies, reserves, warranties, and impairment decisions conservative enough?
- Are capitalized R&D, software, cloud, content, implementation, or customer-acquisition costs sensible, or are operating expenses being pushed into assets?
- Are depreciation, amortization, and maintenance-capex assumptions economically sensible?
- Are tax rates, pension assumptions, and deferred items understandable and not doing hidden earnings work?
- Are lease, supplier-finance, factoring, securitization, or other financing arrangements being treated as financing rather than disguised operations?
- Is stock-based compensation treated as a real cost?
- Are internal-control, audit, restatement, or segment-reporting concerns absent?
- Does management rely more on owner economics or on EBITDA and adjusted narratives?

Hard-fail items:

- Material restatement or internal-control concern
- Recurring "one-time" charges that look structural
- Contract assets, receivables, or channel inventory trends that make revenue quality suspect
- Capitalization abuse or reserve-release dependence
- Capitalized operating costs that materially flatter current economics
- Opaque acquisition accounting or promotional add-backs
- Refusal to expense real economic costs
- Auditor, disclosure, or segment opacity that makes economics unknowable

Rule:

- **Pass** if the statements appear to describe economic reality plainly enough to underwrite.
- **Watchlist** if one narrow accounting item needs verification but the rest of the record is clean.
- **Archive** if aggressive accounting, opacity, or weak controls materially undermine trust in the numbers.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 2. Owner Earnings And Cash Conversion

Questions:

- Can owner earnings be estimated conservatively from reported numbers?
- Are maintenance capex and required working-capital needs understood rather than assumed away?
- Do cash from operations, free cash flow, and owner earnings broadly support the earnings story over a cycle?
- Are lease, pension, warranty, restructuring, legal, tax, or reserve costs treated as real recurring economics where appropriate?
- Is the business usually owner-earnings positive in normal years?
- Are cash conversion swings explainable by the business model, or are they warning signs?

Rule:

- **Pass** if owner earnings are reasonably estimable and cash conversion supports the business story.
- **Watchlist** if one important item such as maintenance capex, working capital, or cash taxes still needs work.
- **Archive** if reported earnings materially overstate owner economics or cash conversion is structurally weak without a convincing reason.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 3. Returns On Capital And Margin Quality

Questions:

- Are ROIC, ROE, and return on tangible capital attractive without undue leverage?
- Are returns stable enough through the cycle to be economically meaningful?
- Are returns on incremental capital acceptable?
- Are gross, operating, and free-cash-flow margins resilient enough for the business type?
- Are the returns real business economics, or mostly the product of leverage, tax quirks, temporary mix, or accounting treatment?
- If returns are mediocre, is that because the business is poor, temporarily depressed, or heavily reinvesting at good prospective returns?

Rule:

- **Pass** if returns and margins are good enough, real enough, and stable enough to support valuation.
- **Watchlist** if the current level is depressed or noisy but the normalization case is specific and testable.
- **Archive** if economics are persistently weak, leverage-dependent, or too unstable to anchor valuation.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 4. Balance Sheet, Liquidity, And Permanent-Loss Risk

Questions:

- Can the company survive a recession, credit freeze, or operating shock without permanent owner impairment?
- Are debt maturities, covenants, refinancing needs, or floating-rate exposures manageable?
- What is the fixed-versus-floating, secured-versus-unsecured, and short-term-versus-term funding mix?
- Are leases, pensions, legal liabilities, guarantees, supplier finance, environmental liabilities, or other obligations material?
- Is reported liquidity real, or is cash trapped, pledged, or operationally unavailable?
- Are derivatives, contingent liabilities, or off-balance-sheet vehicles material and understandable?
- Is leverage a tool, or is it the main reason the economics look acceptable?
- For financials and insurers, are regulatory capital headroom, liquidity, asset quality, reserves, underwriting discipline, and asset-liability matching understandable?

Rule:

- **Pass** if the balance sheet is conservative enough for the business type and downside case.
- **Watchlist** if leverage is probably manageable but one stress, covenant, reserve, or funding issue still needs resolution.
- **Archive** if debt, liquidity, hidden liabilities, reserve risk, or asset quality can create permanent impairment.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 5. Per-Share Economics, Dilution, And Retained-Capital Translation

Use the numerical record here. Intent, biography, and governance design belong primarily to Management Underwriting unless the numbers force a different conclusion.

Questions:

- Have revenue, earnings, owner earnings, and value grown per share, not just in aggregate?
- Has share count been stable, shrinking, or rising?
- Is stock-based compensation modest and sensibly structured, or is it a recurring owner cost and dilution machine?
- Have buybacks occurred at sensible prices?
- Have acquisitions improved per-share value, or mainly increased size and adjusted EBITDA?
- Has retained capital likely created at least one dollar of per-share value for each dollar retained over a reasonable period?

Rule:

- **Pass** if per-share economics are favorable and retained capital appears value-creating.
- **Watchlist** if the picture is mixed but one specific capital-allocation question can still be resolved.
- **Archive** if dilution, buybacks, acquisitions, or retained-capital results are clearly owner-destructive.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 6. Cyclicality, Normalization, And Forecast Reliability

Questions:

- Are current earnings, margins, credit losses, or working-capital flows above, below, or near normal?
- What must normalize, and what should be treated as structurally changed?
- Are volume, price, mix, input cost, credit, reserve, and utilization assumptions explicit?
- Can a low, base, and high owner-earnings range be estimated without heroic assumptions?
- Does the normalization case depend on mean reversion, management promises, refinancing, or favorable macro?
- What would make the normalized range wrong?

Rule:

- **Pass** if normalized economics can be estimated conservatively enough to support valuation.
- **Watchlist** if one or two specific normalization items still need evidence.
- **Archive** if economics are too cyclical, too opaque, or too dependent on heroic recovery assumptions to value responsibly.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 7. Business-Type Fit And Specialist Financial Standard

Questions:

- Is this best analyzed as an asset-light compounder, a good predictable business, an asset-heavy business, a cyclical, a financial/insurer, a roll-up, or a special structure?
- Are the chosen financial metrics appropriate to that business type?
- For financials and insurers, are capital, funding, reserves or credit quality, and asset-liability risks understandable enough to underwrite?
- For roll-ups or serial acquirers, are acquired economics separated from organic economics?
- For asset-heavy businesses, is replacement capex and inflation reality reflected?
- For negative-working-capital or float businesses, is the financing advantage durable and owner-friendly?

Rule:

- **Pass** if the correct financial lens is being used and the key specialist risks are understandable enough.
- **Watchlist** if one specialist item still needs verification.
- **Archive** if the wrong lens is required to make the business look acceptable, or if specialist risks make value unknowable.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

## 8. Ready For Valuation And Position Size

Questions:

- Can valuation be anchored on normalized owner earnings, asset value, spread earnings, or another defensible measure?
- Is financial uncertainty narrow enough to set a reasonable value range later?
- Can Valuation and Position Size use a sensible reinvestment-rate and incremental-return assumption, or is that still guesswork?
- What can valuation safely assume, and what must it refuse to assume?
- Is the remaining debate mainly about price and required return, not accounting cleanliness or survivability?
- If the company is not ready for valuation, what single missing financial fact matters most?

Rule:

- **Pass** if the financial record is clean enough and the normalized economics are knowable enough to value.
- **Watchlist** if one specific financial fact or condition still blocks valuation.
- **Archive** if financial uncertainty is too large for valuation to be more than guesswork.

**Result**: Pass / Watchlist / Archive

**Notes**:

---

# Part VIII. What Must Be True / What To Verify

This replaces broad "financial assumptions." Do not forecast for the sake of forecasting. List the few financial things that must be true for the stock to deserve Valuation and Position Size.

Good examples:

- Reported earnings are reasonably close to owner earnings.
- Maintenance capex is not materially above depreciation over time.
- Working-capital releases are not being mistaken for durable cash generation.
- Returns on capital are not leverage-driven.
- Stock compensation is a manageable owner cost, not a hidden transfer.
- The balance sheet can survive a bad year without forced dilution.
- Retained capital is still creating per-share value.
- Current margins are below normal for temporary rather than structural reasons.

Use no more than seven items. If the thesis needs more than seven major financial things to be true, the economics are probably too fragile.

# Part IX. Munger Error-Prevention Checks

## 1. Inversion

- What is the strongest financial reason this investment fails?

**Answer**:

- What could permanently impair owners even if revenue holds up?

**Answer**:

- What accounting or financing assumption is doing most of the work in the attractive case?

**Answer**:

- What number am I most tempted to over-trust?

**Answer**:

- What would make this an immediate Archive?

**Answer**:

## 2. Psychology And Bias Audit

Check any that apply.

- I am using a low multiple as proof of cheapness.
- I am using EBITDA instead of owner earnings because it looks better.
- I am treating a temporary working-capital release as recurring cash flow.
- I am accepting adjusted earnings without reconciling the adjustments.
- I am assuming margins mean-revert without evidence.
- I am understating maintenance capex because it is hard to estimate.
- I am ignoring dilution because EPS still rises.
- I am treating debt as manageable because maturities are not immediate.
- I am giving management credit for acquisitions without per-share proof.
- I am trusting a tax rate, reserve, pension, or impairment assumption I do not fully understand.
- I am forcing an industrial template onto a financial company, or the reverse.
- I am assuming valuation can solve a financial-quality problem.

Most important bias risk:

**Answer**:

- Which reported metric is management, the board, or lenders most motivated to defend?

**Answer**:

**Answer**:

- What usually happens to businesses with this return profile and capital intensity?

**Answer**:

**Answer**:

- If this is a roll-up or serial acquirer, what is the base rate for per-share value creation after acquisitions slow?

**Answer**:

- If this is cyclical, where are we in the cycle and what are normalized owner earnings?

**Answer**:

**Answer**:

# Part X. Financial Pattern And Next Valuation Standard

Pick the category based on the economics, not on the story.

## Category Options

### Asset-Light Compounder

Traits:

- High returns on capital without much leverage
- Strong owner-earnings conversion
- Modest maintenance capex
- Limited dilution
- Durable reinvestment or cash-distribution flexibility

Financial standard:

- Can pass with a narrower margin of financial error only if accounting is clean and incremental returns are real.

Valuation must respect:

- Do not understate stock compensation, reinvestment needs, or normalization risk just because the business is high quality.

### Good Predictable Business

Traits:

- Acceptable returns on capital
- Reasonable cash conversion
- Manageable leverage
- Moderate reinvestment needs
- Per-share economics are sound but not extraordinary

Financial standard:

- Needs stable owner earnings, a survivable balance sheet, and no major accounting concern.

Valuation must respect:

- Demand a real return from the current price; do not pay compounder multiples for merely decent economics.

### Asset-Heavy / Reinvestment-Heavy Business

Traits:

- High ongoing capex
- Material depreciation and replacement needs
- Inflation and capacity matter
- Cash conversion can differ materially from accounting earnings

Financial standard:

- Pass only if maintenance capex, replacement economics, and normalized returns are understood.

Valuation must respect:

- Use owner earnings after real reinvestment needs, not EBITDA or headline EPS.
- Treat capex-to-depreciation, working-capital drag, and asset replacement as economics, not footnotes.

### Cyclical / Commodity / Balance-Sheet-Sensitive Business

Traits:

- Margins and returns move with the cycle
- Working capital swings are material
- Downside survivability matters heavily
- Peak earnings are dangerous

Financial standard:

- Pass only if normalized or trough-aware economics and downside balance-sheet resilience are credible.

Valuation must respect:

- Do not capitalize peak margins or temporary cash releases.

### Financial / Insurer

Traits:

- Value depends on underwriting, credit quality, reserves, capital, funding, liquidity, and asset-liability management

Financial standard:

- Pass only if capital, reserving or credit quality, funding, and asset quality are understandable enough to underwrite.

Valuation must respect:

- Normalize leverage, credit costs, and regulatory-capital headroom; do not rely on headline ROE or industrial shortcuts.

### Roll-Up / Serial Acquirer

Traits:

- Reported growth depends heavily on acquisitions
- Adjusted metrics are prominent
- Goodwill and intangibles build over time
- Per-share value creation is the real test

Financial standard:

- Pass only if organic economics, integration costs, dilution, and retained-capital results are credible.

Valuation must respect:

- Do not give full credit to acquisition-adjusted numbers without per-share proof.

### Fragile / Promotional / Over-Leveraged

Traits:

- Reliance on refinancing, asset sales, aggressive adjustments, or narrative support
- Thin liquidity
- Weak owner earnings
- Management emphasis on presentation over economics

Financial standard:

- Usually Watchlist or Archive.

Valuation must respect:

- A low multiple does not repair weak economics or weak trust.

**Financial Pattern Result**: Asset-Light Compounder / Good Predictable / Asset-Heavy / Cyclical-Commodity / Financial-Insurer / Roll-Up / Fragile
